meta.title: Aqueduct — a maintained data dependency for agents
meta.note: Numbers are from real runs. Re-capture anytime with `npx tsx scripts/demo.ts`.

scenes.rule: THE OPEN WEB IS UNDER SIEGE
scenes.narration: The open web runs on a quiet army of free databases. DOAJ — the Directory of Open Access Journals — indexes twenty-three thousand of them. It's a small non-profit.

render.text: DOAJ — Directory of Open Access Journals
22,940 journals · journal-level metadata · run by a non-profit, on donations

scenes.narration: In 2025, AI crawlers hammered it so hard — traffic up nine hundred and sixty-eight percent in a single day — that it had to hide behind a wall. The problem: that wall now blocks legitimate AI agents too.

render.text: 2025: AI-crawler traffic +968% in one day → servers buckling
DOAJ's response: put the data behind Cloudflare

scenes.narration: Watch. An agent asks DOAJ for the data directly, and gets a 403. The door is closing on the very people open data was meant to serve.
Aqueduct fixes this from the supply side. The builder downloads the data once — past the wall, like a human would — and runs a single command.
It profiles the file, builds a safe query interface, and runs an eval gate — no model, fully deterministic. Twenty-three thousand journals become a Tap.
That's the whole job. No server to write, no pipeline to babysit. They serve it — and now they have a metered, agent-payable API that takes the load off their origin and pays them per query.

render.text: GET /query    paid   · $0.0001 / row, settled on Tempo

scenes.rule: SAME TASK, SAME MODEL — WITH vs WITHOUT AQUEDUCT
scenes.narration: Now the payoff. Same question, same model, two agents. A researcher's question: find a free, fast, peer-reviewed medical journal to publish in. One agent has the Aqueduct Tap. The other is on its own.

render.text: TASK  diamond-OA (no-APC) Medicine journals, CC BY, plagiarism-screened,
<12-week review — ranked by output. Needs the whole 23k corpus.

scenes.narration: On its own, the agent runs straight into the wall. The bulk CSV — blocked. The API — blocked. It tries the harvesting endpoint, a scraping tool, a web fetch...
Every door is shut. After four and a half minutes, twenty-six turns, and a dollar fifty of compute, it gives up and guesses from memory. And the answer is wrong.
The Aqueduct agent never touches DOAJ. It reads the Tap's schema, writes one constrained query, pays a fraction of a cent per row over MPP, and answers correctly — in under a minute.

render.text: answer:  Memorias do Instituto Oswaldo Cruz   ✓ CORRECT
time: 56s    ·   cost: $0.28   ·   5 turns    ·   data: paid per row (MPP)

scenes.narration: Five times faster. Five times cheaper. And the difference between a correct answer and a confident hallucination — decided entirely by whether the agent could reach clean, complete data.

render.text: data      one paid MPP query     blocked at the origin

scenes.narration: Here's the trick: we don't sell the data. The data is free. We sell never having to host it, refresh it, or get crushed by it — and a metered side-door that pays the operator while serving the agent.

render.text: the data is free — we sell maintained, metered ACCESS
operator: paid per query + origin offloaded
agent: clean, complete, fresh data — one query, pay per row

scenes.narration: Alchemy for the open-data long tail. Paid per query, agent-native, settled on Tempo. Aqueduct.

render.text: a maintained data dependency for apps and agents
· npx aqueduct onboard · pay per row over MPP · non-custodial on Tempo
